docs.title: BERT Document Classifier
docs.text: A high-performance NLP pipeline for automated document classification using the BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) architecture.

docs.title: 🎯 Overview
docs.text: This project aims to provide a robust, production-ready solution for categorizing documents with high accuracy. Originally developed for the Smart India Hackathon 2023, this implementation focuses on optimized preprocessing and scalable deployment.

docs.title: 🚀 Key Features
docs.text:
• Context-Aware Classification: Leverages BERT's bidirectional understanding of language.
  • Optimized Pipeline: 40% faster preprocessing through batching and efficient tokenization.
  • API-First: Built with FastAPI for high-performance inference.
  • Containerized: Fully Dockerized for seamless deployment on AWS EC2 or other cloud providers.

docs.title: 🛠️ Tech Stack
docs.text:
• Language: Python 3.11+
  • Model: HuggingFace Transformers (BERT)
  • API: FastAPI
  • Inference: PyTorch
  • DevOps: Docker, AWS EC2

For detailed development instructions, see DEV_README.md.

docs.title: Changelog (CHANGELOG.md)
docs.text: All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file. The format is based on Keep a Changelog, and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Initial project directory structure.
  • Comprehensive documentation suite (README, DEV_README, DECISIONS, AGENT, CHANGELOG, FINAL).
  • Project subdirectories (src, tests, data, models).
  • Python virtual environment and core dependencies (transformers, torch, fastapi, uvicorn).
  • Initial boilerplate in src/main.py with FastAPI and BERT tokenizer integration.

docs.title: Architectural Decisions (DECISIONS.md)
docs.text: All significant architectural and technical decisions are logged here.

docs.title: 2026-05-03: Initial Project Structure
docs.text:
• Decision: Adopt a multi-file documentation strategy (README, DEV_README, DECISIONS, AGENT, CHANGELOG, FINAL).
  • Reason: User preference for heavy documentation and clear separation of concerns between user-facing docs, developer docs, and AI context.
  • Status: Accepted.

docs.title: 2026-05-03: Base Model Selection
docs.text:
• Decision: Use bert-base-uncased from HuggingFace.
  • Reason: Industry standard for general-purpose document classification with a good balance between performance and resource consumption.
  • Status: Proposed.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11 or higher
  • pip and venv
  • Docker (optional, for containerization)

docs.title: Local Development
docs.text:
1. Create Virtual Environment: 
    python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # Windows: venv\Scripts\activate
  2. Install Dependencies: 
    pip install transformers torch fastapi uvicorn scikit-learn

docs.title: 🏗️ Project Structure
docs.text:
BERT-Document-Classifier/
├── src/                # Source code
├── data/               # Datasets (gitignored)
├── models/             # Local model checkpoints
├── tests/              # Unit and integration tests
├── AGENT.md            # AI Agent guidelines
├── DECISIONS.md        # Architectural decisions
└── CHANGELOG.md        # Semantic versioning log

docs.title: 🧪 Testing
docs.text:
Run tests using pytest: 

pytest

docs.title: 📦 Deployment
docs.text:
Build the Docker image: 

docker build -t bert-classifier .

docs.title: How-to: Manage Environments and Port Configurations
docs.text: This guide provides practical steps for managing the local development environment and resolving common configuration conflicts.

docs.title: Activate the Project Environment
docs.text: To ensure you are using the correct library versions and Python interpreter, always work within the scoped virtual environment.

docs.title: Windows (PowerShell)
docs.text: .\bert-classifier-env\Scripts\activate

docs.title: Linux / macOS
docs.text: source bert-classifier-env/bin/activate

docs.title: Configure a Custom API Port
docs.text: By default, the API service runs on port 8081 to avoid collisions with standard web services (often on 8000 or 8080). You can override this setting using the PORT environment variable.

docs.title: Temporary Override (Windows PowerShell)
docs.text: $env:PORT=9000; python src/main.py

docs.title: Temporary Override (Linux/macOS)
docs.text: PORT=9000 python src/main.py

docs.title: Update Project Dependencies
docs.text:
When a new package is required, follow this workflow to ensure the bert-classifier-env remains consistent.

  1. Activate the environment as shown above.
  2. Install the package: 
    pip install <package-name>
  3. Verify the installation: 
    pip show <package-name>

docs.title: Troubleshoot Port Conflicts
docs.text:
If you see an error like [Errno 10048] error while attempting to bind on address, the port is already in use by another project.

  1. Identify the process (Windows): 
    netstat -ano | findstr :8081
  2. Action: Either kill the conflicting process or use a different port for this project as described in the "Configure a Custom API Port" section above.

docs.title: Tutorial: Your First Steps with BERT and FastAPI
docs.text: This tutorial will guide you through the process of setting up the project and running your first text-processing task using a BERT tokenizer. By the end of this lesson, you will have a live API service that can translate human language into the numerical tokens used by modern language models.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Before starting, ensure you have the following installed: - Python 3.11 or higher - A terminal/command prompt (PowerShell is recommended for Windows users)

docs.title: Step 1: Initialize the Environment
docs.text:
To keep this project's dependencies isolated and avoid conflicts with other Python projects on your machine, we use a project-specific virtual environment named bert-classifier-env.

  1. Open your terminal in the project root directory.
  2. Activate the environment: 
    # Windows
.\bert-classifier-env\Scripts\activate
    You should now see (bert-classifier-env) at the beginning of your command prompt.

docs.title: Step 2: Launch the API Service
docs.text:
We use FastAPI to serve our model logic. The main entry point is located in src/main.py.

Run the following command: 

python src/main.py

You should see output indicating that the server is running on http://0.0.0.0:8081.

docs.title: Step 3: Test the Tokenizer
docs.text:
Now that the service is live, we can verify that BERT is correctly processing text. The tokenizer breaks down sentences into "sub-words" and assigns each a unique ID from its pre-trained vocabulary.

  1. Open your web browser.
  2. Navigate to the following URL: http://localhost:8081/test-tokenizer?text=I love engineering

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
You should receive a JSON response similar to this: 

{
  "text": "I love engineering",
  "tokens": ["i", "love", "engineering"],
  "token_ids": [1045, 2293, 5030]
}

This confirms that: - The FastAPI server is correctly routing requests. - The BERT Tokenizer is successfully loaded and operational. - Your Local Environment is configured correctly.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Congratulations! You have successfully deployed a basic NLP service. In the next tutorial, we will explore how to load and clean external datasets for model training.

docs.title: Project Vision & Final Goals (FINAL.md)
docs.text: This document defines the "Definition of Done" for the version of the project we want to achieve.

docs.title: 🏁 The Ultimate Goal
docs.text: A production-grade, highly optimized document classification service capable of handling diverse document types (PDF, Text, Images via OCR) with >90% accuracy and sub-200ms inference time.

docs.title: 🎯 Key Milestones for "Final"
docs.text:
• [ ] Model Excellence: Fine-tuned BERT or RoBERTa model achieving state-of-the-art performance on our target dataset.
  • [ ] Auto-Scaling Inference: Deployed on Kubernetes or AWS Lambda/ECS with auto-scaling based on request volume.
  • [ ] Observability: Complete monitoring dashboard (Grafana/Prometheus) tracking accuracy, latency, and drift.
  • [ ] Active Learning Loop: Mechanism to flag low-confidence predictions for human review and re-incorporate them into training.
  • [ ] Multi-Modal Support: Integrated OCR (Tesseract or AWS Textract) to handle scanned images and PDFs.

docs.title: 📈 Success Metrics
docs.text:
• Accuracy: >90% on hold-out test set.
  • Latency: P99 < 300ms.
  • Scalability: Handle 100+ concurrent requests.
